# Preface to the Fifth Edition

```{=html}
<p class="fst">A dozen years ago the first edition of this work appeared. Since then much has changed in things and in minds, so much so that here and there within Social Democracy itself the question arose whether its programme, and therefore this commentary upon it, had not already become outdated and irreconcilable with the facts.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>There is no doubt that the earlier editions of the present work are outdated in some points, for formal reasons, because they rest upon material that has been superseded by new facts and new reports. They are outdated above all in that they mostly drew their statistical illustrations from the occupational census of 1882, which has since been entirely pushed into the background by that of 1895. In the new edition I have, of course, put more recent figures in place of the old ones. <a href="#vorwort-04-n1" id="vorwort-04-f1" name="vorwort-04-f1">[1]</a></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But not merely the illustrations of the train of thought—no, the train of thought itself is supposed to be completely outdated and proven incorrect, so that an essential alteration of its programme is demanded of Social Democracy.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>I have accordingly examined the present work closely, but have found nothing of importance to change, except in one point, of which I shall yet come to speak. All the criticisms of the "theory of immiseration" and the "theory of catastrophe" have not led me to change one iota, and indeed for the very reason that in my work there is no mention of either of these "theories." They are theories that were read into the Erfurt Programme, despite all protests, only some years after its composition. Precisely at the time of the origin of the Erfurt Programme, the probability that the proletariat would conquer political power without catastrophe in some countries—for example, England—was greater than it is today. Marx himself had conceded for England the possibility of a peaceful development. If he did not believe in this possibility for the great states of the European continent, that was not due to any particular theory of catastrophe, but to insight into the particular character of state power on the continent of Europe. This did not amount to any theory of catastrophe, any more than the fact that one observes a thunderstorm gathering constitutes a theory of the thunderstorm. Since my work develops only the fundamental principles of the views of Social Democracy, not the foundations of its tactics for particular cases, I had no cause whatsoever to set forth and defend any theory of catastrophe here.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>And just as little did I have any theory of immiseration to develop. At the time of the composition of the Erfurt Programme, the consistent Marxists had long since agreed that the emancipation of the proletariat would be brought about not by rising misery, but by the growing class antagonism and the class struggle of the proletariat arising from it. Precisely in this overcoming of the theory of the immiseration of the masses proper to pre-Marxist socialism, through the theory of the class struggle, we already saw at that time one of the greatest achievements of Marxism. The recognition of the tendency inherent by natural necessity in capital to increase the sum of misery, of pressure, of exploitation, was important from this standpoint because this tendency made it possible to comprehend the necessity of the steady extension and intensification of the class struggle. But it occurred to none among us to infer from this the necessity of an increasing degradation of the proletariat. The theory of immiseration plays no role in this work on the <em>Erfurt Programme</em>. On its account, just as little as on account of the theory of catastrophe, did I need to "revise" so much as a single sentence.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Alongside these two theories, another theory—one that Marx actually did put forward—was also vigorously doubted: his theory of crises. This theory was built upon the experiences of half a century. Further experiences of some decades, which followed its formulation, had brilliantly confirmed it. Now, all at once, in 1898 the theory was declared false; because at that time three—yes, literally three—full years of prosperity had set in. Had German Social Democracy at that time allowed itself to be impressed by this criticism and correspondingly revised the passage of the Erfurt Programme dealing with crises, it would have been faced with the agreeable task of revising this passage anew after two years. Whoever once grants to such short-winded criticisms—which as a rule are nothing but fleeting notions and moods—a decisive influence over conviction and programme, will never again get out of revising; he becomes the plaything of events instead of the master surveying them, who makes them serve his great, immovably held aims.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only on one point did I have to somewhat qualify what was said in the earlier editions: in the expectations concerning the decline of the small-scale enterprise in agriculture. Over the last two decades the dissolution of the peasant small-scale enterprise has not proceeded as rapidly as it once did; in places it has even gained ground. In 1892 this was not yet so clearly apparent. Here I now had to express myself more cautiously than at that time. But that is also all. There is not the slightest reason to acknowledge, in place of the old tendency, a new, opposite tendency toward the displacement of the large-scale enterprise by the small-scale enterprise in agriculture. For that, the available data are far too indeterminate. They do not indicate a changed direction of development, but rather a stagnation of the development hitherto, in so far as the size of the enterprise is concerned. The changes that have taken place during the last two decades in the land area of the individual categories of enterprise are only insignificant, and they do not everywhere proceed in the same direction. In some regions the large-scale enterprise recedes, in others it makes progress. Moreover, the period in which this stagnation becomes noticeable is still far too short to entitle us to draw, from such faintly pronounced facts, conclusions that would overturn the experiences of a century. Were we to let ourselves be led into premature conclusions here, then in the agrarian question we should easily fare as many revisionists have fared in the crisis question. Finally, however, it must be remarked that with the concentration of capital, as Marx conceived it, not only the continued existence but even a certain increase of the small-scale enterprise in individual branches of production is compatible, and indeed not merely in agriculture, but also in industry and in commerce. It is precisely when one thinks in the sense of the Marxian dialectic that one will easily comprehend this increase. Every tendency produces counter-tendencies, which strive to abolish it. But even where they succeed in this, they thereby bring about not a mere return to the condition as it existed before the domination of the abolished tendency, but rather they produce something essentially new. Thus, for example, the misery that capitalism by natural necessity imposes upon the proletariat produces the proletariat's struggle against misery. But there, where the proletarian class struggle becomes strong enough to push back the misery produced by capital, the result is by no means a pre-capitalist workers' idyll. Further, however, when the proletariat strives to organise itself and thereby to shift the power relation that exists between the individual wage labourer and the individual capitalist, this striving in turn produces on the other side the urge toward the organisation of the employers. Where now the employers' association confronts the workers' organisation, the old power relation between the individual wage labourer and the individual employer appears to be re-established. Yet in reality the new power relation is nevertheless quite a different one. However powerful the capitalists may become through their organisations, an employers' organisation may nevertheless no longer deal with the proletarian organisation as the individual capitalist does with the isolated worker. And the consciousness as well as the tactics of the organised workers remain under all circumstances different from those of the isolated ones.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A similar dialectical process also brings it about that from the concentration of capital itself, under certain circumstances, an increase of the small-scale enterprise once again arises. But the new small-scale enterprise is quite a different one from the old, has only externals in common with it, and plays quite a different role both economically and politically.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>As is well known, according to the Marxian view the concentration of capital leads not merely to a dissolution of the traditional independent small-scale enterprise, which is kept going essentially without permanent wage labour, but also to an increase of the reserve army of labour-power. It throws far more labour-power onto the market than the latter can absorb. However, nothing is more mistaken than the view that the whole industrial reserve army consists of the unemployed. On the contrary, these form only a fraction of it, only its highest and lowest strata — here lumpenproletarians, idlers who do not shrink from unemployment, there organised worker-aristocrats whose organisation is strong enough to keep their unemployed "above water" for a time. But the great middle stratum of those who still seek wage labour and find none corresponding to their occupational abilities are compelled to cling to other possibilities of utilising their labour. The only alternative to wage labour, however, is today offered by work in a small-scale enterprise of one's own — the cooperative enterprise does not yet come into consideration as a mass phenomenon. The more rapidly, then, the concentration of capital proceeds, the more rapidly it ruins the original small-scale enterprise and extends the industrial reserve army, the greater the urge among the released labour-power toward the founding or preservation of small-scale enterprises. The displacement of the small-scale enterprise here corresponds to its extension there. The concentration of capital today in Germany removes the small-scale enterprise most rapidly in the illuminants industry, where from 1882 to 1895 the small-scale enterprises decreased by 25 per cent, in the industry of stones and earths (decrease 24 per cent), mining and metallurgy (decrease 84 per cent), the textile industry (decrease 42 per cent). But the same development increased the small-scale enterprises in the commercial trade by 89 per cent, in the insurance trade by 60 per cent, in the trade of lodging and refreshment by 35 per cent. In the manufacture of tobacco and cigars the small-scale enterprises increased from 5465 to 9708, by 78 per cent, in the peddling trade from 202,709 to 312,059, that is 54 per cent. Compared with these figures, the increase figures of the agricultural enterprises under 2 hectares (5.8 per cent) and from 2 to 5 hectares (3.5 per cent) are of a most trivial nature. If one went by the mere figures of statistics, one could also proclaim for commerce, the tavern trade, tobacco manufacture, and a few smaller branches of industry the principle that for them the law of the concentration of capital does not hold. And yet we know quite precisely that it is in force here too.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The new small-scale enterprises that arise from the concentration of capital — home workers, peddlers, dwarf peasants, etc. — are indeed of quite a different nature from those removed by the concentration of capital. The latter rested upon the private property in the means of production, which were the free property of their owners; the new small-scale enterprise receives the most important of its means of production advanced to it by capital, to which it thereby becomes obligated in service — the small peasant on the leased or debt-encumbered soil no less than the home worker to whom the raw material is handed over by the merchant-employer, or the tavern-keeper who is only the agent of the brewery, just as the peddler or petty shopkeeper who obtains the commodities that he distributes on credit.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The old small-scale enterprise constituted a middle stratum – its owner, half capitalist, half wage labourer, stood between the two. The owner of the new small-scale enterprise stands below the wage labourer; he is far more defenceless than the latter, his standard of living is often lower, his working hours are extended longer, and wife and child are exploited far more. The new small-scale enterprise does not constitute a position into which the wage labourer rises, but rather one into which he sinks down – alongside the independent owners of small-scale enterprises who descend to it. The old small-scale enterprise, which is eliminated by the concentration of capital, constituted a competitor of the latter; it stood in hostile opposition to the individual capitalist as a member of the same class of independent producers. The new small-scale enterprise constitutes an object of exploitation for capital and, as a reserve of labour-power for the large-scale enterprise, a precondition for its prosperity; it stands in hostile opposition to the capitalist not as a member of the same class, but as a member of another class oppressed and exploited by him, the proletariat.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The capitalist large-scale enterprise cannot develop unless a reserve of labour-power is at its disposal, which on the one hand presses down upon the wages of the employed wage labourers, and on the other hand permits capital to exploit every economic boom and at times to expand production abruptly through the rapid hiring of new labour-power.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This reserve is offered to it less by the host of the unemployed than by the new sort of, one may say, proletarianised small-scale enterprises. Only in relatively few branches of work has an adequate longer-term unemployment support hitherto been possible. The mass of those who are unemployed for a longer time deteriorate, become unaccustomed to work, and become unusable for exploitation by capital. Quite different are the workers and owners of the proletarianised small-scale enterprises. They are always inclined to flock to large-scale industry as soon as remunerative work is available there, and they come to it with all the willingness to work, the skill, and the submissiveness that the proletarianised small-scale enterprise produces.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>As soon as a longer-lasting era of prosperity makes itself felt, numerous workers from rural and urban small-scale enterprises abandon their previous opportunities for work in order to turn to the large-scale enterprise. The owner of the small-scale enterprise himself is, especially in agriculture, mostly too tightly bound to it to be able, likewise, simply to turn his labour-power to the large-scale enterprise. But he sends it the most energetic and intelligent of his family members, so that the small-scale enterprise is often carried on only by the aged and by children, thus bearing witness most clearly to its new function in the capitalist era, namely to serve as a production site for new workers and a depot for workers who have become superfluous. Not only the industrial but also the agricultural large-scale enterprise increasingly needs this reserve army supplied by the small-scale enterprise; indeed the agricultural large-scale enterprise even more than the industrial. For one of the circumstances that presses upon it, in contrast to the latter, arises from the fact that the new technology, especially the division of labour and machinery, increasingly turns agriculture, where it is carried on capitalistically, into a seasonal trade, which at times needs great masses of workers, but in the intervening period only few workers. Hence the labour shortage of the large landowners, which would be far worse without the reserves of the small peasantry, especially in the east of Germany and beyond its borders, who year in, year out, supply the large-scale enterprises, as migrant workers, with the labour-power so eagerly awaited. Without this surplus of labour-power supplied by the small peasants, the agricultural large-scale enterprise in Germany would be in far greater distress than it is. In this respect, then, under today's organisation of production, the small-scale enterprise is indispensable for the progress of agriculture; not as a technically superior competitor of the large-scale enterprise, but as the most reliable and abundant supplier of proletarians for large landed property. The latter therefore itself even seeks to create small-scale enterprises artificially in order to have more proletarians supplied to it. Thus we see that the concentration of capital itself again produces a need for an increase of small-scale enterprises and promotes it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But is the Erfurt Programme thereby reduced <em>ad absurdum</em>, which speaks of the natural necessity of the downfall of the small-scale enterprise? By no means. It deals only with the downfall of that small-scale enterprise "whose basis is formed by the private property of the worker in his means of production".</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This does not apply, as we have seen, to the new small-scale enterprise, whose most important means of production are owned by capital. The new small-scale enterprise is a thoroughly proletarian formation, whose members increasingly lose all interest in the private property in the means of production, and increasingly arrive at the same class antagonism as the wage-earning proletariat. If the old small-scale enterprise constituted the firmest bulwark of the private property in the means of production and thus of capitalism, the new one constitutes an element of the proletarian antagonism against this private property and thus against capital. Since those active in it are more isolated, more oppressed, more overworked than the workers of the large-scale enterprises, and their economic position also not so simple and clear as that of the actual wage labourers, they are far more difficult to organise and to bring to a consciousness of their situation than the latter; they may under certain circumstances, as strike-breakers and conservative voters, slow down the emancipation struggle of the proletariat, but nowhere any longer do they constitute an element upon which capital could permanently ground its rule. Sooner or later their class interests always drive them to the side of the fighting wage-labouring population.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The old small-scale enterprise, handed down from the heyday of the handicraft, constituted one of the firmest and most indispensable economic foundations of the society of its time. The new, proletarianised small-scale enterprise constitutes one of its waste products, which, under the given social conditions, is just as unavoidable as, say, crime and prostitution, but which, just as little as these, can be a healthy foundation of society. The new small-scale enterprise increasingly becomes a parasitic formation, a makeshift that only burdens society and of which those who live from it easily and gladly rid themselves as soon as the distress ceases. Already today we see how, during every epoch of prosperity, the small-scale enterprises in town and country are abandoned in droves. Should the proletariat once conquer political power and thereby the possibility of organising the whole of production in accordance with its interests, it would above all have to strive to abolish the industrial reserve army. This, however, would lead to a rapid desolation of the small-scale enterprises in most branches of industry, of commerce, of agriculture.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Nothing is more mistaken than the view that socialist production will only become possible once all small-scale enterprises have been absorbed. It would then never become possible, because the concentration of capital does not allow the small-scale enterprise to disappear entirely, but in many cases merely puts a new one in place of the old. The absorption of these new, parasitic-proletarian small-scale enterprises is first made possible by the introduction of socialist production. The latter is the precondition, not the consequence, of the complete disappearance of the small-scale enterprise from all economic spheres in which it has become technically superfluous. Not the complete disappearance of the small-scale enterprise from the enterprise statistics, but its elimination from the production processes that dominate social life, their subjection to capital, which monopolises the means of production and all the advantages of their increasing perfection — these are the preconditions of socialism. That these are growing at the most rapid pace can today be grasped with the hands even by one who is socially and politically blind.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is in this sense that the doctrine of Marxism concerning the concentration of capital, which was taken over into the Erfurt Programme, is to be understood. Understood in this way, this principle not only does not stand in contradiction with the actual facts; rather, it is what first offers the possibility of comprehending them fully. On this point too, just as little as on the others which "critical socialism" objected to, does the fundamental part of the Erfurt Programme require any revision.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is not self-praise when I say this, for the Erfurt Programme is by no means my exclusive work. It was indeed built up on the basis of the draft programme I proposed. But the Erfurt programme commission substantially expanded this draft. In the draft itself, however, those sentences which were later most discussed were taken almost verbatim from Marx's "Capital"; the general part of the programme itself is only a paraphrase of the well-known passage on "The Historical Tendency of Capitalist Accumulation" in "Capital". It is precisely in this that I see the source of the strength of the Erfurt Programme and of its capacity to withstand the changing fashions. As long as no one has succeeded in putting another theoretical foundation of socialism in place of "Capital", so long will the Erfurt Programme also not require any revision of its principles.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="date">Berlin-Friedenau, in May 1904.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="author">K. Kautsky</p>
```

## Notes

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#vorwort-04-f1" id="vorwort-04-n1" name="vorwort-04-n1">1.</a> This Internet edition reproduces the programme commentary according to the first, unaltered edition.</p>
```


---

[↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd)
